pageTitle.value: Tahar Ben Jelloun and Edem Awumey - Video

pageCaption.value: Moroccan-born and now one of France’s most fêted writers, Tahar Ben Jelloun declares that “literature is not reassuring, it’s disturbing”. Edem, from Togo, writing a novel of “dark nights and ghosts”, was the ideal protégé to accompany in the challenging process of creating fiction.

description.value: Rolex Mentor and Protégé in Literature, 2006 - 2007

title.value: Tahar Ben Jelloun and Edem Awumey

text.value: Moroccan-born and now one of France’s most fêted writers, Tahar Ben Jelloun declares that “literature is not reassuring, it’s disturbing”. Edem, from Togo, writing a novel of “dark nights and ghosts”, was the ideal protégé to accompany in the challenging process of creating fiction.

pageTitle.value: Wole Soyinka and Tara June Winch - Video

pageCaption.value: Declaring their mentorship to be “a process of development”, Wole Soyinka, first African Laureate of the Nobel Prize for Literature, encourages his protégée, Tara June Winch, as she agonizes over her writing. When anyone writes, he says, himself included, “it’s all over the place.” It’s the rewriting that follows that marks out a true writer.

WaPageTitle.value: Wole Soyinka and Tara June Winch - Video

metadescription.value: Declaring their mentorship to be “a process of development”, Wole Soyinka, first African Laureate of the Nobel Prize for Literature, encourages his protégée, Tara June Winch, as she agonizes over her writing. pageTitle.value: Mia Couto and Julián Fuks - Video

pageCaption.value: Mia Couto’s mentoring relationship with Julián Fuks was a fusion of interests wrought by their mutual experiences as writers living in former colonies of Portugal. Liberated from his “autofiction” style of writing by his mentor, Fuks found he was able to explore his imagination in a different way.

WaPageTitle.value: Mia Couto and Julián Fuks - Video

pageTitle.value: Hans Magnus Enzensberger and Tracy K. Smith - Video

pageCaption.value: In a mentorship that they describe as an intense and joyful conversation, poets Hans Magnus Enzensberger (from Germany) and Tracy K. Smith (American) explore the nature of poetry – “Everyone has poetry in their head,” Enzensberger proclaims. But their quest goes further, examining history and identity as Smith realizes a longstanding desire to write her family memoir.

WaPageTitle.value: Hans Magnus Enzensberger and Tracy K. Smith - Video

pageTitle.value: Pinchas Zukerman and David Aaron Carpenter - Video

pageCaption.value: World-renowned violinist and conductor Pinchas Zukerman says “the fiddle is synonymous with my existence”. He finds the same commitment in his protégé, young American violist David Aaron Carpenter, from the United States.

WaPageTitle.value: Pinchas Zukerman and David Aaron Carpenter - Video

previewTitle.value: Pinchas Zukerman and David Aaron Carpenter - Video

pageTitle.value: Julie Taymor and Selina Cartmell - Video

pageCaption.value: Julie Taymor, famous for creating spectacular theatre, film and opera, wanted to mentor “someone I can have a dialogue with”. She found her in British director Selina Cartmell, for whom the mentorship is an ideal opportunity for the rare privilege of watching another director at work as Taymor creates a new opera, Grendel.

WaPageTitle.value: Julie Taymor and Selina Cartmell - Video

pageTitle.value: John Baldessari and Alejandro Cesarco - Video

pageCaption.value: Renowned American conceptual artist John Baldessari believes that “art is more than just painting”. His mentorship of Alejandro Cesarco (from Uruguay) is an intense collaboration that combines visuals, text, irony and humour in the creation of a new work of art.
